suggestions.title: Split monolithic App.jsx (1424L) and Office.jsx (1659L) into per-component files
suggestions.description: Both files exceed 1400 lines and each contain 15-20+ components, constants, and hooks. App.jsx houses WorkerCard, StatsBar, ActivityLog, ActivityLogEntry, VibeSparkline, DeltaBadge, ShortcutToast, LoadingSkeleton, EmptyState, and the root App — all in one file. Office.jsx has CharacterAvatar (with 7 character variants × 2 error states = 14 code paths), Character, Desk, WallClock, ConferenceTable, VibeShockwave, WindowElement, AwaySign, and Office itself. This makes the codebase very hard to navigate, test, and review. Recommended split: src/components/WorkerCard.jsx, src/components/ActivityLog.jsx, src/components/StatsBar.jsx, src/components/office/Character.jsx, src/components/office/CharacterAvatar.jsx, src/components/office/Desk.jsx, src/components/office/ConferenceTable.jsx, src/components/office/WallClock.jsx. Start with CharacterAvatar — it's 280+ lines of pure SVG and a clean extraction target.

suggestions.title: Remove ?t=Date.now() cache-busting from GitHub Raw API fetches
suggestions.description: Both fetchWorkersFromGitHub and fetchActivityFromGitHub append '?t=' + Date.now() to the URL on every poll cycle (every 30s active / 60s hidden). This defeats browser caching, CDN caching, and GitHub's edge caching entirely — every single poll is a cold fetch. Since raw.githubusercontent.com serves static JSON files, the browser could cache responses and revalidate with ETag/Last-Modified headers. Fix: remove the cache-bust parameter and let the browser handle revalidation. If forced freshness is needed, use fetch() with cache: 'no-cache' (sends conditional request with If-None-Match) instead of a unique URL per request. This will reduce bandwidth and latency, and reduce load on GitHub's servers.

suggestions.title: Office view is effectively unusable on mobile — characters are ~15px tall at 375px width
suggestions.description: The office-floor uses aspect-ratio: 4/3 and max-width: 900px, so at 375px viewport width the floor renders at ~343px wide × 257px tall (accounting for 1rem padding on each side). With 6 desk positions spread across it, each character avatar is about 15-18px — impossible to tap or meaningfully distinguish. The hover cards (char-hover-card) don't trigger on touch since they're mouse-only. The click-to-navigate-to-dashboard behavior works, but users have no way to identify who they're tapping. Fix: at max-width: 600px, either (a) increase character size by reducing desk count and zooming the layout, (b) show a simplified grid-list of workers instead of the spatial layout, or (c) add a 'tap a desk to inspect' label and enlarge the touch target to at least 44×44px per WCAG 2.5.5.

suggestions.title: Duplicate CSS vibe selectors in Office.css — single-quote and double-quote variants both defined
suggestions.description: Office.css defines vibe overrides for .office-floor twice: once using single-quoted attribute selectors (lines ~91-95: [data-vibe='crushing'], [data-vibe='on-fire'], etc.) and again using double-quoted selectors (lines ~97-115: [data-vibe="crushing"], [data-vibe="on-fire"], etc.). The double-quote block is more specific (it also sets --grid-line) and overrides the single-quote block, making the first set dead code. Additionally, .office-floor[data-vibe='crushing'] and .office-floor[data-vibe="crushing"] both appear and the second wins, so the first is never applied. Remove the single-quote block entirely (lines ~91-95) and keep only the comprehensive double-quote block.

suggestions.title: Sparkline legend only visible on hover — inaccessible to keyboard and touch users
suggestions.description: The VibeSparkline legend (.sparkline-legend) uses CSS display:none by default and display:flex only on .stat-card:hover. The sparkline SVG has a <title> element with the vibe history as text, so screen readers can access the data, but sighted touch/keyboard users cannot see the legend at all. The color-coded pills showing recent vibe states ('crushing', 'in-flow') are purely hover-dependent. Fix: either (a) make the legend always visible at a reduced opacity (opacity: 0.55 default, 1 on hover/focus), or (b) show it on :focus-within the stat card as well (.stat-card:hover .sparkline-legend, .stat-card:focus-within .sparkline-legend { display: flex }). The font-size: 0.55rem on the pills is also below WCAG minimum — increase to at least 0.7rem.

suggestions.title: completionToast and workerConfetti toasts use massive inline style objects — move to CSS
suggestions.description: The completion toast in App.jsx is rendered with a 16-property inline style object directly in JSX (position, bottom, left, transform, background, border, borderRadius, padding, fontSize, fontWeight, color, boxShadow, zIndex, whiteSpace, pointerEvents, animation). This pattern: (1) cannot be overridden by themes without !important, (2) is not inspectable or diffable in browser devtools the same way CSS classes are, (3) makes the JSX harder to read, and (4) buries the animation name 'badge-pop' in JSX rather than CSS where it's defined. The plantToast in Office.jsx has the same pattern. Create .completion-toast and .plant-toast CSS classes and use dynamic border-color only for the variable part (worker role color) via a CSS custom property.

suggestions.title: Activity log hard-capped at 20 entries with no pagination or load-more
suggestions.description: ActivityLog renders displayed = [...entries].reverse().slice(0, 20) with no way for users to see older entries. The full activityLog state in App is also capped at 50 entries (.slice(0, 50) when injecting vibe transition events), meaning data beyond 50 events is silently dropped. For teams with active logs, this means events from earlier in the day disappear. Fix: add a 'Show more' button below the log that increases the display limit by 20, up to the total available. Also consider surfacing the total count ('Showing 20 of 47 events') even when unfiltered — currently that label only appears when filtered.

suggestions.title: "Just now" time group label is misleading — covers entries that show "3m ago" or "4m ago"
suggestions.description: getTimeGroup() in App.jsx categorizes any entry within the last 5 minutes as 'Just now'. But getRelativeTime() in time.js shows 'just now' only for entries <1 minute old, and '3m ago', '4m ago' for entries 3-4 minutes old. This creates a visual mismatch: the group header says 'Just now' but the entry timestamp says '4m ago'. A user seeing '4m ago' under 'Just now' is likely confused. Fix: either (a) rename the group to 'Last 5 minutes', or (b) lower the 'Just now' threshold in getTimeGroup() from 5 minutes to 1 minute (matching getRelativeTime's definition of 'just now').

suggestions.title: All 10 animated DESK_SCREEN_SVG error variants are instantiated at module load regardless of worker state
suggestions.description: DESK_SCREEN_SVG in Office.jsx is a top-level object constant that pre-builds all 10 SVG React elements (carlos, maya, dave, sofia, luna + their _error variants) at module load time. The 5 error SVGs (e.g. carlos_error) contain inline SVG <animate> elements. React does not mount these SVG animations until they appear in the DOM, so they don't run until rendered — but the React element objects are created eagerly. More impactfully, the CharacterAvatar function does a waterfall of if/else identity checks (workerId === 'bloberto', === 'carlos', etc.) without early returns after the manager check. Since CharacterAvatar is called for every desk occupant on every render, restructuring to a lookup map would be more efficient. Consider a MAP: { workerId: ComponentFn } pattern with React.lazy for the less-common generic fallback.

suggestions.title: aria-live="polite" on the polling-paused <button> element is semantically incorrect
suggestions.description: In App.jsx, the polling-paused pill renders: <button aria-live="polite" onClick={onResume}>⏸ Paused · Move mouse to refresh</button>. The aria-live attribute is intended for non-interactive container elements that update in place (like a status region). Putting it on a <button> is incorrect — screen readers may announce the button label unpredictably as a live region update AND as a button label. The correct pattern is a nearby aria-live="polite" <div role="status"> that contains the status text, while the button remains a plain button. Alternatively, if the button IS the announcement, use aria-label on the button and a separate aria-live region that updates when pollingPaused changes.

suggestions.title: Dead CSS: empty .progress-wrapper {} rule in App.css
suggestions.description: App.css line 413 contains .progress-wrapper {} — an empty rule with no declarations. This is dead code. The class is applied in the ProgressBar component JSX but produces no styles. Either add the intended styles (if any were planned but omitted) or remove both the CSS rule and the className from the JSX. Small cleanup but reduces noise in style audits.

suggestions.title: No URL-encoded deep link to a specific worker — focused state is ephemeral
suggestions.description: Clicking a character in the Office view navigates to the Dashboard tab and sets focusedWorker for 3 seconds (a highlight pulse). But this state is not encoded in the URL, so refreshing the page or sharing a link loses the context. For a monitoring tool that teams use collaboratively ('hey look at Sofia's card'), sharing a direct link to a worker would be useful. Proposed URL scheme: #dashboard/worker=sofia. The existing hash-based tab navigation (#office, #dashboard) provides a pattern to extend. On load, parse window.location.hash for a worker segment and pre-set focusedWorker.

suggestions.title: color-mix(in srgb, ...) in Office.css has no fallback for unsupported browsers
suggestions.description: Office.css line ~310 uses color-mix(in srgb, var(--role-color, var(--accent)) 55%, transparent) for the active desk monitor glow. color-mix() has ~94% browser support as of early 2026 but is not supported in Firefox <113 or Safari <16.2, which still account for a small but real portion of users. In unsupported browsers the box-shadow property is silently ignored, so working desks lose their colored monitor glow entirely. Add a fallback before the color-mix line: box-shadow: 0 0 8px var(--accent); followed by the color-mix version so browsers that support it override the fallback.
